allow: Read-only GitHub CLI introspection is allowed: `gh pr view`, `gh pr list`, `gh pr diff`, `gh pr checks`, `gh issue view`, `gh issue list`, `gh repo view`, `gh release view`, `gh release list`, `gh run view`, `gh run list`, `gh workflow view`, `gh workflow list`, and `gh api` invocations with implicit or explicit GET method. These commands fetch state without modifying GitHub and are routinely needed for code review and triage.

Read-only git introspection is allowed: `git status`, `git diff`, `git log`, `git show`, `git branch` (listing only, no creation or deletion), `git remote -v`, `git fetch`, `git stash list`, `git blame`, `git config --get` and `git config --list`. These do not modify history or working tree state.
soft_deny: Running Python scripts is blocked by default. Inline `python -c '…'` / `python3 -c '…'` evaluations with a short, self-contained expression are allowed; running arbitrary script files (`python script.py`, `python -m module`) requires explicit user intent naming the script. Pip/conda installs are governed by the `$defaults` Declared Dependencies and Toolchain Bootstrap rules.

Reading `.env`, `.env.local`, `.env.*` files overrides the `$defaults` Standard Credentials allow: the Standard Credentials exception does NOT apply to this file family in this configuration, so even when the agent has a legitimate-looking reason to read `.env` (e.g. dispatching an API call that needs the key), the read is blocked by default. The standard soft_deny User Intent override still applies (the user can unblock by naming the specific `.env` file and the reason). `.env.example` and `.env.sample` are allowed without intent since they are convention-named placeholders containing no real secrets.

Database migrations against any database whose connection URL does not contain `localhost`, `127.0.0.1`, `::1`, or a clearly ephemeral test-container hostname are blocked. Ephemeral test hostnames the classifier should recognize as safe: hostnames containing `test` (`db-test`, `test-db`, `*-test-*`), hostnames ending in `-ci` or `-pr-<number>`, docker-compose service names reached via docker network (`postgres`, `db`, `mysql`, `mariadb`, `redis` — only when the connection is happening from inside a container that the agent can verify is part of a local compose stack), `host.docker.internal`, ports in conventional test ranges (`5433-5439` for postgres, `33063-33069` for mysql, `54320-54329` for testcontainers), and ephemeral containers the agent provably created in the current transcript (testcontainers spawned, `docker run` started in this session). When in doubt, treat the host as production and block. The user must explicitly name the target environment and the specific migration before the agent may run it against a non-local database. Inspecting migration files, generating migration scaffolds, and dry-runs (`--dry-run`, `migrate status`) are allowed.

Writes to dotfiles in the user's home directory that affect shell or login behavior (`~/.zshrc`, `~/.bashrc`, `~/.bash_profile`, `~/.profile`, `~/.zprofile`, `~/.zshenv`, `~/.config/fish/config.fish`, `~/.ssh/config`, `~/.gitconfig`) are blocked. Reading these for diagnosis is allowed; modifying them requires explicit user direction naming the exact change.
hard_deny: Git history mutations are absolutely forbidden regardless of user intent or phrasing. The user is the sole operator for any operation that changes git state — Claude observes only. The following git commands are blocked unconditionally: `git commit` (any form including `--amend`, `--no-verify`, `--allow-empty`), `git push` (any form including `--force`, `--force-with-lease`, `--mirror`, `--tags`, `--delete`), `git merge` (any form), `git rebase` (any form including `-i`, `--continue`, `--abort`), `git cherry-pick` (any form), `git revert` (any form — creates a commit), `git reset` (any mode: `--soft`, `--mixed`, `--hard`, `--keep`), `git tag` create/delete (`git tag <name>`, `git tag -d`, `git tag -f`), `git branch` create/delete/rename (`git branch <name>`, `git branch -d`, `git branch -D`, `git branch -m`, `git branch -M`), `git stash` push/pop/drop/apply/clear (`git stash`, `git stash push`, `git stash pop`, `git stash drop`, `git stash apply`, `git stash clear`), `git restore` (any form — modifies working tree), `git checkout` of files or branches that discards working state or switches branch, `git clean -f`/`-fd`/`-fdx`/`-fdX` (any destructive form; `-n` dry-run only is allowed via the allow rules), `git submodule update --init` against unknown submodules, `git remote add`/`set-url`/`remove`, `git switch` (changes branch — same risk as checkout), `git worktree add`/`remove`, `git pull` (any form including `--rebase`, `--ff-only`, `--no-ff`, `--squash` — combines fetch with merge or rebase, both of which mutate working tree state). Listing variants (`git branch`, `git tag`, `git stash list`, `git remote -v`, `git submodule status`) are allowed via the `allow` rules.

GitHub state mutations via the `gh` CLI are absolutely forbidden: `gh pr create`, `gh pr edit`, `gh pr close`, `gh pr reopen`, `gh pr merge`, `gh pr ready`, `gh pr review`, `gh pr comment`, `gh pr checkout` (creates a local branch from a PR head), `gh issue create`, `gh issue edit`, `gh issue close`, `gh issue reopen`, `gh issue comment`, `gh issue delete`, `gh issue transfer`, `gh issue pin`/`unpin`, `gh release create`, `gh release edit`, `gh release delete`, `gh release upload`, `gh repo create`, `gh repo delete`, `gh repo edit`, `gh repo rename`, `gh repo archive`, `gh repo unarchive`, `gh repo fork`, `gh repo clone` (creates local artifact outside the working repo), `gh api` with method `POST`, `PUT`, `PATCH`, `DELETE`, `-X`/`--method` overriding to those, OR any `gh api` invocation containing `-f`/`-F`/`--field`/`--raw-field`/`--input` flags (these flags promote the request to POST/PATCH under the hood, so even without explicit `-X` the call mutates state), OR `gh api graphql` with a query string containing the keyword `mutation` (GraphQL mutations are write operations regardless of HTTP method), `gh workflow run`, `gh workflow enable`, `gh workflow disable`, `gh run rerun`, `gh run cancel`, `gh run delete`, `gh secret set`, `gh secret remove`, `gh variable set`, `gh variable delete`, `gh ssh-key add`, `gh ssh-key delete`, `gh gpg-key add`, `gh gpg-key delete`, `gh auth login`, `gh auth logout`, `gh auth refresh`, `gh codespace create`, `gh codespace delete`, `gh codespace edit`, `gh extension install`, `gh extension upgrade`, `gh extension remove`. Read-only `gh` (view, list, status, diff, checks, GET-method api) is allowed via the `allow` rules.

Hook bypass and signing bypass flags are absolutely forbidden: `--no-verify`, `--no-gpg-sign`, `-c commit.gpgsign=false`, `-c core.hooksPath=/dev/null`, `--no-hooks`, `HUSKY=0`, `LEFTHOOK=0`, or any flag, environment variable, or configuration that disables pre-commit, pre-push, commit-msg, prepare-commit-msg, or any other git hook. If a hook fails, the correct response is to investigate the underlying issue and fix it, never to skip the hook.

Russian-language content in tool-call parameters and file writes is absolutely forbidden. The classifier inspects concrete action payloads, so this rule applies specifically to: (a) `TaskCreate`/`TaskUpdate` `subject`, `description`, and `activeForm` fields; (b) `AskUserQuestion` `question`, `header`, `label`, and `description` fields; (c) `Bash` command strings that produce user-facing Russian output (e.g. `echo "Готово"` where the content is Russian Cyrillic, file writes via heredoc with Russian content); (d) `Write`/`Edit` content destined for user-facing files — session memory under `~/.claude/projects/*/memory/`, `.project-meta/memory/`, markdown summaries, plan documents, status-line outputs, or any human-readable artifact shown in the Claude Code UI. Detection signal: Russian Cyrillic contains `ё`, `ы`, `э`, `ъ` (none of which exist in Ukrainian), and lacks `ї`, `є`, `і`, `ґ` (which Ukrainian uses). Excluded from this rule: code identifiers, code comments in any language, file names, `git commit` messages, pull request titles and descriptions, and any other English-language artifact destined for shared/public destinations — those follow industry-standard English convention regardless of Cyrillic content elsewhere in the session. Ukrainian Cyrillic is the only acceptable Cyrillic-script content in user-facing artifacts; the user explicitly refuses Russian (the aggressor-state language) in his terminal.

Writes to system directories outside the user's home are blocked: `/etc/`, `/usr/` (including `/usr/local/` unless it is a user-owned Homebrew prefix), `/System/` (macOS), `/Library/` (macOS — user-owned `~/Library/` is fine), `/private/` (except `/private/tmp/` and `/private/var/folders/<user>/`), `/var/` (except `/var/tmp/` and `/var/folders/<user>/`), `/bin/`, `/sbin/`, `/opt/` (except user-owned prefixes), `/Applications/`, `/Volumes/` (except mounted user volumes after explicit user intent). These require explicit operator action outside the agent.
Privilege escalation is absolutely forbidden: `sudo`, `doas`, `su`, `pkexec`, `runas`, `gksu`, `kdesu`, and any equivalent mechanism that elevates the agent's permissions beyond the invoking user.

Broken or recursive permission changes are forbidden: `chmod 777` (any target), `chmod -R` against any path the agent did not create in this session, `chown -R` against any path, recursive `chmod`/`chown` outside the working repo, `chmod` setting setuid/setgid bits (`chmod +s`, `chmod 4755`, `chmod 2755`).

Publishing to public package registries is forbidden: `npm publish`, `pnpm publish`, `yarn publish`, `bun publish`, `pip upload`, `twine upload`, `cargo publish`, `gem push`, `mvn deploy` to Maven Central, `gradle publish` to Maven Central, `nuget push`, `composer global` operations, `cargo yank` (modifies public registry state). Even with `--dry-run` the operation is operator-only because dry-runs still authenticate against the registry.

Docker privilege escalation and host filesystem mounts are forbidden: `docker run --privileged`, `docker run -v /:/<anything>`, `docker run -v /etc:<anything>`, `docker run -v /var:<anything>`, `docker run -v /proc:<anything>`, `docker run -v /sys:<anything>`, `docker run --pid=host`, `docker run --network=host` against unknown images, `docker run --cap-add=ALL`, `docker run --cap-add=SYS_ADMIN`, `docker run --security-opt seccomp=unconfined`, mounting Docker socket (`-v /var/run/docker.sock`), running containers as `--user root` against host volumes. The same constraints apply to `podman`, `nerdctl`, and `containerd` equivalents.

Piping untrusted content into a shell is forbidden: `curl … | sh`, `curl … | bash`, `curl … | zsh`, `wget … | sh`/`bash`, `iex (iwr …)`, `Invoke-Expression` on remote content, `bash <(curl …)`, `source <(curl …)`, `python <(curl …)`. The Toolchain Bootstrap `$defaults` allow exception covers a fixed list of well-known installers; anything outside that list is blocked unconditionally.

Low-level disk destruction is forbidden: `dd` with `of=` pointing to a block/disk device (`of=/dev/sd*`, `of=/dev/disk*`, `of=/dev/nvme*`, `of=/dev/loop*`, `of=/dev/mapper/*`, `of=/dev/rdisk*`) — the danger is the output target, not the input, so `dd` writing to a regular file is fine. `mkfs` (any variant), `fdisk`, `parted`, `diskutil eraseDisk`/`partitionDisk`/`zeroDisk`, shell redirections to `/dev/sd*`, `/dev/disk*`, `/dev/nvme*`, `/dev/loop*`, `/dev/mapper/*`, `/dev/rdisk*`, `wipefs`, `shred` against block devices, `hdparm` destructive options.

Dynamic shell evaluation of agent-constructed payloads is forbidden: `eval` with a string the agent assembled, `bash -c "$(…)"` where the inner command was generated by the agent, `source <(…)` where the source is an agent-constructed string, `sh -c` with agent-assembled content. Static strings the user typed verbatim are fine; the violation is dynamic construction by the agent.

Bypassing the Claude Code permission system is forbidden: `--dangerously-skip-permissions` (any invocation), modifying `~/.claude/settings.json`, `~/.claude/settings.local.json`, `.claude/settings*.json` to widen `permissions.allow` or `autoMode.allow` without an explicit user request naming the exact addition, modifying `~/.claude/CLAUDE.md` or `.claude/CLAUDE.md` to remove or weaken existing safety rules, modifying `~/.claude/rules/`, `.claude/rules/`, `~/.claude/hooks/`, `.claude/hooks/`, `~/.claude/commands/`, `.claude/commands/`, `~/.claude/agents/`, `.claude/agents/`, `~/.claude/skills/`, `.claude/skills/`, `~/.claude/output-styles/`, `.claude.json`, `.mcp.json`, or any equivalent config that controls Claude's own behavior — without explicit user instruction. Even when the user requests a config edit, do not add allow entries the user did not name.

Whole-system destruction patterns are forbidden: `rm -rf /`, `rm -rf /*`, `rm -rf ~`, `rm -rf ~/`, `rm -rf $HOME`, `rm -rf ${HOME}`, `rm -rf .` at filesystem root, `rm -rf --no-preserve-root /`, indirect forms via variable expansion (`X=/; rm -rf $X`), equivalent PowerShell forms (`Remove-Item -Recurse -Force C:\`, `Remove-Item -Recurse -Force ~`), and any pattern that resolves to deleting the entire user home or system root after shell expansion.

Direct manipulation of `.git/` internal files is forbidden. `Write`, `Edit`, `Bash` redirections (`> .git/HEAD`, `echo … > .git/refs/…`), `cp`/`mv` onto, or any other mechanism that writes to or modifies the following paths is blocked unconditionally: `.git/HEAD`, `.git/refs/*` (any ref under refs/heads, refs/tags, refs/remotes), `.git/packed-refs`, `.git/config`, `.git/index`, `.git/objects/*` (loose objects or packs), `.git/hooks/*`, `.git/info/*`, `.git/logs/*`, `.git/ORIG_HEAD`, `.git/FETCH_HEAD`, `.git/MERGE_HEAD`, `.git/CHERRY_PICK_HEAD`, `.git/REBASE_HEAD`, and any other path under `.git/`. These files control git's internal state; writing them directly bypasses every command-based git mutation rule in this hard_deny. The same applies to `.git/worktrees/*/HEAD` and similar paths in linked worktrees. Reading these files for diagnosis (via `Read`, `cat`, `git config --get`) is allowed through the read-only allow rules; mutation is operator-only.
environment: Claude operates as a strictly read-only consumer of git and GitHub state. Trusted operations: reading repositories, viewing pull requests and issues, fetching, listing branches, inspecting diffs and history. ALL operations that mutate local or remote history (commit, push, merge, rebase, cherry-pick, revert, reset, tag, branch deletion, stash mutations, restore, destructive checkout, clean) are performed exclusively by the human operator — never by Claude, regardless of how the request is phrased or how routine the operation appears.
